Loopholes and Gaps in Policy STRUCTURE

No dedicated textile waste management regulation; imports sometimes used to fill gaps in domestic 
recycling, risking landfill overflow and poor traceability.

Infrastructure is limited; most sorting is manual or semi-automated, with only a few pilot projects for 
advanced sorting. Lack of scalable, formal collection and segregation systems.

No Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for textiles; manufacturers not legally accountable for post-
consumer waste.

No national targets for textile waste reduction or recycling; export focus does not incentivize domestic 
circularity.

No dedicated textile waste policy; current oversight is fragmented and lacks coordination among 
agencies.
Fragmented supply chain, lack of traceability, and transparency; informal sector handles 80% of waste.

No formal earning model or social security for waste workers; informal sector dominates, leading to poor 
labor conditions.

I Highest Environmental Burden: The route from Panipat to Dwarka, Rajkot, has the highest CO₂ emissions, driven by its long distance (1,387.2 
km) and high fuel consumption. This route also contributes significantly to PM₁₀ and NMVOC emissions, increasing human health impacts 
(DALY)O

I Interesting Fact: Despite its short distance (103.7 km), the Panipat to Delhi route has a disproportionately high PM₁₀ emission per kilometer 
due to frequent stops and urban traffic, amplifying health impacts in densely populated areasO
I The 22-Tyre Truck produces 71% emissions than 6-Tyre Truck on the same route.

I African Market Dominance: While UAE has the highest number of 
shipments (302), African countries collectively represent the 
largest export market by both volume and value. Kenya ($8.97M) 
and Uganda ($6.93M) are the top two destinations by valueO

I High-Value Asian Market: Japan stands out with relatively few 
shipments (80) but very high total value ($5.14M), indicating 
higher value goods being exported there compared to most other 
destinationsO

I Extreme Value Distribution: There's a significant disparity in 
export values - the top 10 countries account for approximately 
65% of total export value ($43.28M out of $63.62M), while many 
countries have minimal export values under $1,000O

I Shipment Size Variations: Countries like Honduras, Haiti, and 
Spain have very few shipments but relatively high average values 
per shipment (over $79,000 each), suggesting bulk exports or 
high-value goods.

emissions are substantialO

I The voyage produces approximately 2,160 kg of PM₂.₅ equivalent, which contributes to air pollution in port cities and coastal areas, affecting local 
air quality and public health

Recycled Materials, Sorted Post Consumer, 
Vintage, Hand loom Products made out of waste.

Consumption-Recycling Relationship: Changing the fundamental paradigm from "recycling as waste management" to 
"recycling as resource conservation"
Value Recognition: Shifting perception of textile waste from liability to valuable resource

Circular Economy Metrics: Establishing clear indicators that measure circularity rather than just throughput
Value Chain Integration: Creating shared objectives across the entire textile lifecycle
Industry Cluster Development: Points where natural organization among recyclers could create collective capacity

Extended Producer Responsibility: Framework requiring brands to account for end-of-life handling
Transparency Requirements: Mandatory disclosure standards for material flows
Labor Standards Enforcement: Consistent application of worker protection regulations

Places to intervene in a system - By D. Meadows
Leverage Points

Material Traceability Systems: Points where tracking technology could be implemented
Consumer Awareness Channels: Information pathways connecting consumption choices to recycling realities
Cross-Stakeholder Dialogues: Forums for knowledge exchange between fragmented actors

“Recycling as resource conservation” rather than “waste management” encourages consumers & industry to view textile 
waste as valuable.As more actors adopt this mindset, investment in recycling, innovation in design-for-recycling,& consumer 
participation increase exponentially, reinforcing the circular model-improved economic returns, which further incentivizes 
sustainable design & product recovery systems.

Perceptions that recycled materials are inferior can depress demand.This reduces the return on recycling infrastructure, 
potentially deterring further investment.
Industry Adoption Delay: Planning, funding, and building advanced recycling infrastructure

Sorting Efficiency Nodes: Critical bottlenecks in waste classification processes
Transportation Network Optimization: Physical movement pathways of materials
Processing Facility Design: Layout and equipment configuration improvements

Material Storage Infrastructure: Facilities to manage fluctuating waste volumes
Skill Development Programs: Building human capital resilience in the workforce
Government Support for recycling industry through tax breaks, subsidies, technical support or easier procedures.

Lighthouse Model presents a blueprint of completely automated textile recycling facility aiming for net-zero emissions, integrating Industry 
4.0 technologies like automation, AI, and IoT. It details the recycling process flow and the specific automated equipment used at each stage, 
alongside outlining the layout of the facility, including sensors and other necessary infrastructure. Furthermore, it addresses energy and 
resource circularity through wastewater treatment, air filtration, and thermal recycling, and presents estimates for total energy consumption 
and throughput. It also compares the automated facility's metrics to a conventional manual one and discusses various carbon offsetting 
methods to achieve the net-zero goal, including on-site renewable energy generation and carbon credit purchases.

Phase 2: Enhanced Automation (EA)
Phase 3: Fully Automated (FA)

India’s textile sector is at the cusp of a transformative shift, and through comprehensive ground-level research and systems-based diagnostics 
across key textile hubs like Panipat, Kandla, and Amroha, we have mapped the operational, social, and environmental challenges impeding the 
sector’s transition to a circular economy. This groundwork—rooted in stakeholder engagement, material flow analysis, policy gap identification, 
and labor condition assessments—has directly informed the development of our final outcome: a fully operational and future-ready model of a 
net-zero, automated textile recycling facility. The model proposes a data-backed, contextually tailored blueprint that embodies the principles of 
sustainability, scalability, and inclusion.

The light house  integrates advanced automation technologies including AI-based sorting systems, MES platforms, Digital Twin technology, and 
renewable energy infrastructure, enabling high material recovery, energy efficiency, and real-time traceability. Beyond technology, this model also 
addresses the critical need for structured regulatory intervention. It is aligned with national ambitions such as Digital India and PM MITRA, while 
proposing a strong case for the implementation of a dedicated textile waste management policy. Key policy features supported by our model 
include Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), material traceability mandates, standardized processing protocols, and subsidies for clean 
technology adoption. The system is also designed to operate within updated legal and labor frameworks that ensure safe working conditions, 
gender equity, and formal recognition of previously informal labor roles.

It represents the convergence of rigorous field research, technological innovation, and policy foresight. It offers a replicable framework to 
industrialize textile recycling in a manner that is environmentally restorative, socially just, and economically viable- step toward reshaping India’s 
textile sector into a global leader in circular economy practices.

This illustrates the roadmap from semi-automation to enhanced and full automation in textile recycling culminating in a net-zero, high-efficiency 
facility. By integrating advanced tech and circular practices, it positions India as a global leader in sustainable textile recycling.
Phased Implementation and Modular Equipment Upgrades

Quarterly audits for material purity (98%) and emissions (net-zero via 
100–500 kW solar) using MES software.

Semi-automated phase with basic PM MITRA standards (e.g., waste 
management), enhanced phase with VDI 4.0 (digital twins), and fully 
automated phase with full Industry 4.0 integration.
Develop a MindSphere-based dashboard for realtime regulatory reporting

Form public-private partnerships with textile brands (e.g., Reliance, 
Aditya Birla) to co-fund automation via EPR obligations.
Secure low-interest loans from SIDBI for equipment and solar installations.

Monetize net-zero operations through carbon credit markets to offset 
costs

Implementing predictive maintenance via IoT sensors to ensure 90–
95% uptime

Launch 6–12 month training programs in PLC programming, NIR system 
operation, and MindSphere, partnering with institutes like NIFT and 
ATDC, inspired by SIPTexs model.

Hold town halls to communicate automation benefits (e.g., safer roles, 
skill growth) and transition plans.

Invest in local infrastructure (e.g., schools, healthcare) near PM MITRA 
parks, as seen in community driven models like NexTiles
Partner with manufacturers to use recycled fibers in new products.

Develop certification schemes for recycled textile products to build 
consumer trust.
Collaborate with FICCI and CITI to advocate for recycling policies
Implement textile waste collection systems, inspired by EU models

Deploy IoT sensors to monitor throughput (1–5 t/h), purity (98%), and 
energy use, integrated with MindSphere.

Pilot test enhanced automation (3 t/h) to identify bottlenecks. Share 
performance data with policymakers to refine incentives

Partner with brands and retailers for pre and post-consumer waste 
streams.
Promote mono-fiber use in textile production to simplify recycling.

Retrain 50% of workers for operator/technician roles at 3 t/h; offer 
voluntary exit packages or redeployment to other textile sectors at 5 t/h.

Develop nationally recognized certifications for automation skills to 
enhance employability

Provide tax incentives for modular, energy efficient equipment under 
Textile Mission 2.0.

Expand Technology Up-gradation Fund Scheme (TUFS) to cover 
recycling equipment.

Reduce import duties for automation technologies (e.g., NIR, AI 
sorters)

Subsidize training costs through Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas 
Yojana (PMKVY).
Mandate upskilling programs in PM MITRA park approvals.
Establish a Textile Automation Skill Council to standardize curricula.

Introduce procurement policies favoring recycled textiles in 
government projects. 
Subsidize research into high-value applications for recycled fibers

Integrate textile recycling metrics into EPR regulations, holding 
producers accountable for waste.
Offer fast-track environmental clearances for net-zero facilities.

Mandate real-time compliance reporting for PM MITRA park 
tenants.

Create a Green Textile Fund to subsidize scale-up costs for net-

infrastructure.

plans.

Building resilient circular value systems in 
India’s Textile Sector

Redefining Waste: Building Resilient Circular Value Systems in India’s 
Textile Sector investigates the pressing environmental and social 
challenges posed by textile waste in India. As global fast fashion 
continues to generate over 92 million tons of waste annually, India has 
emerged as a key player in the global textile waste economy both as a 
producer and a major importer of discarded garments from the US, EU, 
and East Asia.

The study is grounded in extensive field research across key recycling 
hubs including Kandla, Panipat, Amroha, and Tirupur. It maps the flow 
of imported textile waste through sorting centers, resale markets, 
downcycling units, and recycling factories. Despite the presence of 
advanced machinery in some facilities, the sector remains fragmented 
and heavily reliant on informal labor.

Using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and Material Flow Analysis (MFA), 
the research quantifies emissions, material leakage, and labor 
conditions across the value chain. It introduces the “Lighthouse 
Model”, a digitally enabled framework that integrates Industry 4.0 
technologies such as AI and IoT into circular textile ecosystems and 
framing it to digital twin technology to enhance transparency, 
efficiency, and scalability.

The study culminates in a policy roadmap that addresses textile waste 
imports, emission standards, and labor governance. It advocates a 
systemic shift toward a circular, resilient, and climate-responsible 
textile economy—positioning India as a global leader in sustainable 
and inclusive fashion systems.

different—sometimes it 
makes my head spin. 
But I keep going to pay 
my son's school fee.

H Escorts her daughter to school before 
heading to the textile sorting unit near 
the Kandla portS
H Takes a shared auto-rickshaw if money 
allows; otherwise walks 30–40 minutes.

H Eats a packed meal of rotla and leftover 
sabzi while sitting on a torn gunny sackS
H No designated lunch space; often near 
the open area of the godownS
H Restrooms are shared, unsanitary, often 
without water.

H Leaves the facility, stopping to buy 
vegetables or groceries from roadside 
vendorsS
H Sometimes has to stand in queues for 
subsidized ration if it’s her quota dayS
H Walks back home carrying heavy bags

H Lights a kerosene lamp if there’s no powerS
H Cooks dinner with minimal ingredients—
khichdi, rice, or dal if affordableS
H Supervises her children’s homework, helps her 
mother-in-law take medication.

H Washes the day’s clothes by hand with cold 
water—no detergent on most days, or soap 
scrapsS
H Scrubs utensils from the day’s mealsS
H Sweeps and mops the small room; shakes 
out mats for the children to sleepS
H Fills water buckets for the next morning if 
supply resumes late at night.

H After the short lunch break, Rekha returns to exactly the same sorting line she leftS
H Each bale weighs roughly 45‑50 kg; the team of eight women—including Rekha—
opens, shakes, and grades about three bales every hourS
H Dust rises as each bale is broken; without masks the women sometimes wrap the 
ends of their dupattas across their nosesS
H A floor supervisor (“mistri”) clocks their tally on a notebook: by the end of a normal 
day the line will have processed 20–22 bales—nearly a metric ton of cloth—just in 
Rekha’s section.

H Starts with sorting imported second-
hand clothesS
H Clothing is separated by fiber type, 
color, and reusabilityS
H The air is heavy with fabric dusS
H No safety masks, gloves, or mechanized 
tools.

“Better than the village”
Migration to Kandla is seen as a step up from 
agrarian precarity, especially for women.

Textile waste is normalized — not framed as 
dangerous or toxic, especially among workers

There's little discourse of environmental justice or 
labor rights; survival and routine override critique.

Dye runoff, fiber dust, and chemical 
exposure are seen as “part of the job”, 
not framed as environmental hazards.

This discursive absence makes 
organizing difficult, as grievances are 
individualized.

Panipat work, though exhausting, is 
framed as reliable “factory” work.

Material Identification: Intuitively identify fabric types (wool, 
polyester, denim, cotton blends) through touch, stretch, and 
texture—a skill developed through experience.

Kandla:  Godowns and open-air sheds, often with poor ventilation. 
Dust, mold, and fiber particles fill the air, especially during bale 
opening. No air purification or temperature control, making it 
unbearable during peak summer and monsoon.

Panipat:  Many units are located in old industrial structures with 
minimal upgrades. Workers face heat, noise, and sharp machinery 
in the absence of proper insulation or sound barriers. Some 
factories are semi-mechanized, increasing injury risks from open 
gear machinery.

Other Risks:  Lack of formal contracts, 10-12 hours daily work, 
Inadequate availability of drinking water, unsanitary shared toilets

Rapid Sorting: Teams in Kandla sort 2–3 bales/hour, with 
women picking through hundreds of garments in real-time under 
time pressure.

Challenges: No formal certification or recognition of skills.
No structured training for new workers; risk of injury and inefficiency.
Skill stagnation due to repetitive tasks and absence of mobility 
pathways.

Peer Mentoring: Most learning is on-the-job, passed down 
from older workers or neighbors.

My day starts at six and ends past 
midnight. I cut hundreds of 
sweaters into pieces, and return to 
wash clothes and make dinner. My 
son’s TB treatment takes 
everything I earn, but I dream of 
seeing him study again one day.

I help unload bales from 
trucks that come in 
from Delhi and Gujarat.
I dropped out of school after 10th 
because we needed another 
income. The room I work in has one 
bulb and no fan—still, it pays more 
than college ever would.

We pick through rejected 
clothes with our bare hands, 
separating threads and 
stuffing sacks. There’s no 
ventilation, and no breaks if 
we’re behind target.

I stand beside a shredder 
machine all day, feeding cloth 
and watching my fingers. Once I saw a 
man lose two fingers, but we got no 
compensation. I live with five others in 
one room—no space, but cheaper than 
going back home.
